BENCHMARK: BTC buy & hold

      
DATA: Daily OHLCV, exact cache reproduction

      
BACKTEST: 8.04 years, 2018-06-01 onward

    

  


  

    

      

    


    




  

    01
    
Executive Summary

    Backtest: 2018-06-01 → 2026-06-16 · 8.04 years
  


  

    
Core Finding

    
"Three rounds of risk-control patches on a long-only signal cut max drawdown by 80%
      — but never escaped its structural ceiling. Flipping the same signal to a cross-sectional,
      dollar-neutral construction broke through it."

    

  


  

    This report documents the development of two crypto trading strategies built on the
    same core signal — proximity to a 20-day high — applied through two
    fundamentally different lenses. The first lens, absolute momentum, asks "is this asset
    strong relative to its own recent history?" and goes long-only on assets that qualify. Three
    iterations of this approach (v1 → v2 → v3) progressively patched its risk controls,
    cutting max drawdown from −70.4% to −14.4% — but never escaped
    a structural ceiling on returns and a quiet, persistent correlation to Bitcoin. The second lens,
    cross-sectional momentum, asks "is this asset strong relative to its peers right
    now?" and goes simultaneously long the strongest names and short the weakest, dollar-neutral.
    Same signal, different exposure architecture — and the second framing produced
    materially better risk-adjusted returns.
  


  


  

    

      
Equity Curves, All Strategies (log scale, normalized to 1.0)

      

    

    

      
01.1The Narrative Arc

      

        v1 (13 assets, Top-6, minimum-variance sizing) posted the highest raw CAGR
        (46.6%) but also the deepest drawdown (−70.4%) — full capital deployment at
        all times meant that when most positions exited in a crash, the survivor(s) absorbed
        100% of the book's risk.
      

      

        v2 widened the universe and added a trailing stop, but also loosened the exit
        window — a net loss of edge (CAGR fell to 35.8%, Sharpe to 0.88) for a partial
        drawdown improvement. A genuine "more complexity, less alpha" data point.
      

      

        v3 fixed the real architectural flaw: inverse-volatility sizing with per-slot
        cash, so unfilled or de-risked slots simply sit in cash rather than reflowing into
        survivors. Drawdown collapsed to −14.4% and Sharpe rose to 1.29 — but CAGR
        fell to 15.0%, below BTC buy-and-hold's own 31.2%. The long-only architecture had
        hit a ceiling: it can only express "this looks strong," never "that looks weak."
      

      

        The pivot to cross-sectional market-neutral construction — long the top-4,
        short the bottom-4 by the same momentum score, dollar-neutral — broke through that
        ceiling: Sharpe 1.65, CAGR 42.7%, with near-zero realized correlation to BTC
        (−0.12). It is not strictly dominant (its −24.6% max drawdown is deeper than
        v3's), but its risk-adjusted profile, diversification, and resilience in tail events
        (Section 9) are decisively stronger.
      

    

  


  

    METHODOLOGY NOTE
    All four strategy variants are reproduced from their original source notebooks/scripts and
    validated bit-perfect against each notebook's own saved execution outputs, using the
    author's exact cached Binance OHLCV data (20 USDT pairs, 2017–2026). Market-cap
    proxy = price × circulating supply (current supply held static); see Section 3 and
    Section 10 for the limitations this introduces.
  





  

    02
    
Why Crypto Behaves Differently

    Market structure facts that motivate every design choice below
  


  

    Crypto markets share surface-level mechanics with equities — order books, OHLCV
    candles, momentum and mean-reversion effects — but four structural differences drive
    almost every design decision in both strategies below.
  


  

    

      
NO CLOSE, NO CIRCUIT BREAKERS

      

        Crypto trades 24/7/365 with no exchange-mandated trading halts. A leverage cascade that
        would trigger a circuit breaker on an equity exchange instead runs uninterrupted —
        which is exactly what happened on Oct 10, 2025 (Section 9), when roughly $19B in
        leveraged positions were liquidated within hours with no pause mechanism anywhere in the
        pipeline. Both strategies below rely on daily close-to-close signals, meaning they
        are structurally blind to intraday moves of this kind until the next bar.
      

    

    

      
STRUCTURALLY HIGH BASELINE CORRELATION

      

        Across the 20-asset universe used in this report, the average pairwise correlation
        between daily returns is  — at all
        times, not just during stress. This is far higher than typical equity-sector
        baseline correlations and is the central empirical fact behind Section 5's
        "diversification illusion": holding 6–8 different long-only crypto positions is
        much closer to one concentrated BTC-beta position than the headline diversification
        would suggest.
      

    

    

      
LEVERAGE, PERPETUALS & LIQUIDATION CASCADES

      

        Perpetual futures with 25–125x leverage are the dominant venue for crypto price
        discovery, and forced liquidations of over-levered positions are a recurring source of
        violent, mechanical (not fundamentals-driven) price moves. Three of the five tail events
        in Section 9 (May 2021, FTX, Oct 2025) were leverage-unwind events at their core. The
        market-neutral strategy (Section 6) implicitly shorts via this same derivatives
        infrastructure in live trading, which introduces funding-rate carry costs not modeled
        here (Section 10).
      

    

    

      
YOUNG, NARRATIVE-DRIVEN, COUNTERPARTY-FRAGILE

      

        Crypto's investor base skews retail and narrative-driven (Section 8's DOGE case study),
        its exchanges and stablecoin issuers are unregulated or lightly regulated relative to
        traditional finance, and its history includes multiple exchange/protocol failures that
        erased double-digit percentages of total market value in days (LUNA/UST, May 2022; FTX,
        Nov 2022). These are tail risks with no equities analogue at comparable frequency, and
        they recur often enough in this 8-year backtest window to materially shape both
        strategies' risk architecture.
      

    

  


  
02.1What This Means for Strategy Design

  

    Three design choices in both strategy families trace directly back to these facts: a 200-day
    SMA regime filter and BTC-relative regime overlay (responding to high baseline correlation
    and the absence of circuit breakers — Sections 4–5), a market-cap eligibility
    filter intended to reduce exposure to thinly-traded or failing assets (Section 3, with the
    LUNA case study in Section 8 showing exactly how this can both help and quietly mislead), and
    no use of leverage in either backtest (the market-neutral strategy is unlevered 0.5/0.5
    long/short, even though crypto market structure would technically support much higher
    leverage).
  





  

    03
    
Universe & Data

    20 Binance USDT pairs · daily OHLCV · market-cap proxy ranking
  


  

    All strategies draw from the same point-in-time universe: daily OHLCV candles from Binance,
    cached locally and reproduced exactly for this report. The universe expanded from 13 assets
    (v1) to 20 (v2 onward) as the project progressed; each asset enters the eligible pool only
    once its price history begins, and exits automatically if delisted or if its data feed ends.
  


  

    

      
Asset Listing Timeline (data availability by asset)

      

    

    

      
03.1Market-Cap Proxy & Its Limitation

      

        Daily eligibility for all four strategies is determined by ranking assets on
        price × circulating_supply, using each asset's current
        circulating supply held static across the entire backtest. This is a deliberate
        simplification — true historical market cap requires a paid data vendor —
        and it is generally a reasonable proxy except when an asset's actual supply
        changes dramatically, which is precisely what happened to LUNA in May 2022 (its real
        supply expanded roughly 16,000–18,500× in days as Terra's algorithm
        hyperinflated it to defend the UST peg). Section 8 and Section 10 quantify exactly how
        this distorts the LUNA case study.
      

      

        Two assets, OP (listed May 2022) and ARB (listed March 2023), simply have
        no price history before their listing dates and are therefore never eligible during that
        period — not excluded by any explicit rule, just absent from the data. MATIC's
        cached price history ends in September 2024 (coinciding with the Polygon/MATIC→POL
        token migration), after which it drops out of all eligibility calculations the same way.
      

    

  


  
03.2Universe Detail

  

    

      
        
AssetSymbolFirst AvailableLast AvailableStatusTrading Days

      
      
    

  





  

    04
    
Iteration Log: v1 → v2 → v3

    Same core signal, three rounds of risk-architecture changes
  


  

    Every version shares the identical alpha source: score = close / rolling(20).max()
    — how close today's price is to its own 20-day high. A new 20-day high (score = 1.0)
    triggers a long entry; the position exits after a run of days without a new high, or on a
    regime/eligibility trigger. What changed across three iterations was entirely the
    risk architecture wrapped around that signal — universe size, exit timing, and
    position sizing.
  


  
04.1Parameter Evolution

  

    

      
        
Parameterv1v2v3

      
      
        
Universe size13 assets20 assets20 assets

        
Eligible pool (Top-N by mcap)688

        
Breakout lookback20d20d20d

        
Momentum-decay exit5 consecutive days12 consecutive days5 consecutive days

        
200d SMA regime gate/exitYesYesYes

        
Trailing stopNone15%15%

        
BTC regime overlayNoneYesYes

        
Position sizingMin-variance, ridge-regularized, sum-to-1Min-variance, ridge-regularized, sum-to-1Inverse-vol, per-slot cash, NOT sum-to-1

        
Covariance / vol window20d60d20d (per-asset only)

        
Sharpe0.950.881.29

        
CAGR46.6%35.8%15.0%

        
Max Drawdown−70.4%−61.2%−14.4%

      
    

  


  


04.2v1 — Initial Implementation
Sharpe 0.95CAGR 46.6%Max DD −70.4%

  

    

      
Core Alpha: 20d Breakout

      
Long on new 20-day high; exit after 5 days without one.

    

    
+

    

      
200d SMA Gate

      
Entry only above SMA; forced exit if price crosses below.

    

    
+

    

      
Min-Variance Sizing

      
Ridge-regularized MVP, 20d covariance window, weights sum to 1.

    

  

  

    The known weakness, documented in the original notebook: with no trailing stop, the
    SMA exit is the sole crash defense, and it typically fires only after price is already
    40–70% off its high. Worse, because MVP weights are normalized to sum to 1, if 5
    of 6 positions exit during a crash, the sole survivor inherits the entire portfolio's risk
    budget — maximum concentration at exactly the worst moment. This combination produced
    v1's headline numbers: the highest CAGR of the three (46.6%) but also by far the deepest
    drawdown (−70.4%, worse even than BTC buy-and-hold's −76.6% is barely better).
  


  
04.3v2 — Six Changes, Net Loss of Edge
Sharpe 0.88CAGR 35.8%Max DD −61.2%

  

    

      
Trailing Stop (new)

      
15% stop from the running position high — catches violent moves faster than the SMA can.

    

    
+

    

      
BTC Regime Overlay (new)

      
Altcoin entries blocked when BTC itself is below its own 200d SMA.

    

    
+

    

      
Expanded Universe + Looser Exit

      
20 assets, Top-8, exit window extended 5→12 days, covariance window extended 20→60 days.

    

  

  

    Two genuinely protective changes (trailing stop, BTC overlay) shipped alongside one
    speculative change that didn't pay off: extending the momentum-decay exit from 5 to 12 days,
    on the hypothesis that giving trends more room to develop would improve returns. It didn't
    — days 5–12 of a stalling position carry negative expected return on average, and
    holding through them compressed both CAGR (46.6% → 35.8%) and Sharpe (0.95 → 0.88).
    Drawdown did improve (−70.4% → −61.2%), but the MVP sum-to-1 normalization
    flaw from v1 was untouched — v2 is "safer but worse," a genuine lesson about adding
    complexity that doesn't pay for itself.
  


  
04.4v3 — Fixing the Real Flaw
Sharpe 1.29CAGR 15.0%Max DD −14.4%

  

    

      
Inverse-Vol Sizing + Per-Slot Cash (new)

      
Each position targets a fixed 2% daily vol contribution from its own 1/8 capital slot. Empty or undersized slots stay in cash — never redistributed.

    

    
+

    

      
Exit Window Reverted

      
Momentum-decay exit back to 5 days (v2's 12-day extension reverted).

    

    
+

    

      
Trailing Stop + BTC Overlay Retained

      
Both v2 protections kept; volume-confirmation filter tested and removed (see note below).

    

  

  

    
Inverse-Volatility Position Sizing

    wi = clip( 0.02 / (σi × TOP_N), 0, 1/TOP_N )
  

  

    This is the architectural fix: weights no longer sum to 1. If 7 of 8 slots are empty, the
    survivor is capped at 1/8 of capital, not reallocated to 100%. Max drawdown collapsed from
    −61.2% to −14.4% and Sharpe rose to 1.29, the best risk-adjusted number of the
    three long-only iterations — but CAGR fell further, to 15.0%, below BTC's own
    buy-and-hold CAGR of 31.2% over the same window. The strategy had become genuinely safer,
    but at the cost of giving up most of its edge over simply holding BTC. The notebook also
    records a tested-and-reverted change: a volume-confirmation filter was added experimentally
    and removed because it suppressed entries for smaller/newer assets too aggressively without
    measurably improving signal quality — a real "we tried X, it didn't help" data point.
  


  



    
v1 vs v2 vs v3 vs BTC Buy & Hold — Equity Curves (log scale)

    

  





  

    05
    
Strategy 1 Performance & the Fundamental Flaw

    Five risk overlays, one structural ceiling
  


  

    By v3, the long-only breakout strategy carries five distinct risk-management layers: a
    momentum-decay exit, a 200-day SMA regime gate, a 15% trailing stop, a market-cap eligibility
    filter, and a BTC regime overlay. Each one earns its place — together they cut max
    drawdown by 80% relative to v1. But none of them touch the strategy's one structural
    limitation: it is long-only. It can express "this asset looks strong" by buying it,
    and it can express "I'm not sure" by holding cash. It has no mechanism whatsoever to express
    "this asset looks weak" or to profit when one does.
  


  

    

      
v3 Equity Curve & Drawdown

      

    

    

      
90-Day Rolling Correlation to BTC, All Long-Only Versions

      

    

  


  
05.1The Diversification Illusion, Quantified

  

    A natural objection: v3's measured correlation to BTC (0.39 overall, falling to 0.12 when
    BTC itself is in a bear regime) looks reasonably low — isn't 6–8 different
    long-only positions genuinely diversified? Mostly not, and the reason is structural rather
    than statistical: BTC-bear-regime correlation looks low largely because the BTC overlay
    and SMA gate keep the strategy mostly in cash during those windows, not because the
    positions it does hold are independent of each other. The cleaner test is the average
    pairwise correlation among the 20-asset universe itself, independent of any one
    strategy's exposure rules:
  


  

    

      
Average Pairwise Correlation Across the 20-Asset Universe (60d rolling)

      

    

    

      
05.2Baseline vs. Tail-Event Correlation

      

        

          
WindowAvg. Pairwise Corr

          
        

      

      

        The baseline average pairwise correlation across the full universe is already
         — high by the standards of any traditional
        asset class, at all times. During each of the five tail events studied in Section
        9, it spikes to 0.80–0.96. A basket of 6–8 long-only crypto positions
        is, in practice, much closer to one concentrated, levered bet on overall market direction
        than the position count would suggest — and that concentration is worst precisely
        during the crashes a long-only strategy most needs protection from.
      

    

  


  
05.3The Ceiling This Creates

  

    The clearest evidence of the structural ceiling is simple: v3's CAGR of 15.0% is
    below BTC buy-and-hold's own 31.2% over the identical window, despite all five risk
    layers and despite a vastly better risk-adjusted profile (Sharpe 1.29 vs. 0.75, max drawdown
    −14.4% vs. −76.6%). Every risk control added across v1→v2→v3 reduced
    exposure, and reducing exposure mechanically caps upside in a long-only framework with no
    offsetting short book to monetize the other side of the trade. This is the moment the
    project's narrative pivots: the signal itself — proximity to a 20-day high — was
    never the problem. The exposure architecture wrapped around it was.
  





  

    06
    
The Pivot: Cross-Sectional Market-Neutral Momentum

    Same signal, opposite exposure architecture
  


  

    The fix is not a new signal — it is the same score = close / rolling(20).max()
    momentum measure, re-applied cross-sectionally instead of absolutely. Every day, the
    score is computed for all eligible (Top-8 by market cap) assets, and the question changes
    from "is this asset near its own 20-day high?" to "how does this asset's score rank against
    its peers today?" The top-4 scorers go long; the bottom-4 go short; both legs are
    equal-weighted and the book is dollar-neutral by construction.
  


  

    

      
1. Score Every Eligible Asset

      
score = close / rolling(20).max(), restricted to the Top-8 by market-cap proxy.

    

    
→

    

      
2. Rank Cross-Sectionally

      
Daily rank, ascending and descending, among only the eligible names.

    

    
→

    

      

        
3a. Long Top-4 (M=4)

        
Weight +0.5/M = +0.125 each → 50% gross long

      

      

        
3b. Short Bottom-4 (M=4)

        
Weight −0.5/M = −0.125 each → 50% gross short

      

    

    
=

    

      
Dollar-Neutral Book

      
100% gross exposure, 0% net — daily re-rank, re-rebalance.

    

  


  

    The book only activates when at least 2×M = 8 assets are eligible (so both
    the long and short baskets can be fully populated); otherwise it sits flat. Weights are
    applied with a one-day shift (today's ranks determine tomorrow's positions) to avoid
    look-ahead. TOP_N=8, M=4, LOOKBACK=20 are the parameters used throughout this
    report; Section 10 stress-tests sensitivity to all three.
  


  
06.1What's Missing — By Design, and Honestly

  

    The long-only versions accumulated five risk layers over three iterations. The
    market-neutral strategy has zero: no trailing stop, no SMA regime filter, no BTC
    overlay, no per-position circuit breaker of any kind. It relies entirely on the long-short
    construction itself — and daily re-ranking — to manage systemic risk. That
    asymmetry (5 layers vs. 0) is a deliberate simplification worth stating plainly, not a
    claim that the market-neutral architecture is risk-free. Section 9's tail-event tests and
    Section 10's stress tests probe exactly where this absence of position-level safeguards
    shows up.
  


  

    

      
Long-Only (v3) — 5 Layers

      

        
  Momentum-decay exit

        
  200d SMA regime gate

        
  15% trailing stop

        
  Market-cap eligibility filter

        
  BTC regime overlay

      

    

    

      
Market-Neutral — 0 Layers

      

        
  No trailing stop

        
  No SMA regime filter

        
  No BTC overlay

        
  No per-position circuit breaker

        
  Daily cross-sectional re-rank only

      

    

  


  

    

      
Sharpe Ratio

      
1.65

      
vs. v3's 1.29

    

    

      
CAGR

      
42.7%

      
vs. v3's 15.0% and BTC's 31.2%

    

    

      
Max Drawdown

      
−24.6%

      
deeper than v3's −14.4%, far shallower than v1/v2

    

  


  

    NOT STRICTLY DOMINANT Market-neutral does not beat v3 on every
    single metric — its drawdown is deeper and its drawdown duration is far shorter
    (Section 7). The honest framing is a regime change in risk/return character, not a free
    lunch: substantially higher return per unit of risk, genuine diversification from BTC, at
    the cost of a structurally different (and, as Section 10 shows, transaction-cost-sensitive)
    risk profile.
  





  

    07
    
Head-to-Head Comparison

    All four strategy versions vs. BTC buy & hold and an equal-weight 20-asset basket
  


  

    Full risk-adjusted comparison across the backtest window, including two benchmarks: BTC
    buy-and-hold (the simplest possible crypto exposure) and an equal-weight, daily-rebalanced
    basket of all 20 universe assets (a naive "own everything" diversification baseline).

07.1Return Distributions

  

    Daily return histograms (clipped to ±15% for readability) make the fat-tail
    difference visible directly: v1/v2 carry wide dispersion in both directions, v3 is sharply
    compressed around zero (reflecting how little capital is typically deployed), and
    market-neutral shows a tighter, slightly right-skewed distribution — consistent with
    its much higher Sortino ratio relative to its Sharpe ratio (large gains are less penalized
    than large losses by construction).
  

  

    

      
Daily Return Distribution — v1 vs v2 vs v3

      

    

    

      
Daily Return Distribution — Market-Neutral vs. BTC B&H

      

    

  





  

    08
    
Asset-Level Case Studies

    Three individual cryptocurrencies as a proxy for strategy strengths and weaknesses
  


  

    Aggregate portfolio statistics can hide as much as they reveal. These three single-asset
    deep dives show exactly how each architecture's mechanics played out, day by day, against
    real historical price action.
  


  

    

      

        
 LUNA — The 2022 Death Spiral

        
Collapse window: 2022-05-05 → 2022-05-13 · all four strategies

      

      
AVOIDED / SHORTED — FOR DIFFERENT REASONS

    

    

      

        

          

        

        

          

            
v1 Last Exit (Momentum Decay)

            
$94.55

            
2022-04-08, ~1 month before collapse

          

          

            
v2 / v3 LUNA Exposure, 2022

            
Zero

            
Never ranked inside Top-8 of 20

          

          

            
Market-Neutral LUNA Leg P&L

            
+9.9%

            
contribution, May 1–13 2022

          

          

            
Real Supply Expansion

            
~16,000–18,500×

            
vs. our static-supply assumption: 1×

          

        

      

      

        

          All four strategies came through LUNA's collapse essentially unscathed, but for three
          different reasons, only one of which reflects genuine foresight. v1's last LUNA
          position closed on 2022-04-08 at $94.55, a full month before the de-peg began —
          its momentum-decay exit fired for an ordinary reason (no new 20-day high), and simply
          happened to land before the crash. v2 and v3 never held LUNA at all in 2022: the
          expanded 20-asset universe pushed LUNA's market-cap rank to 9–18, permanently
          outside the Top-8 eligible set — a structural, coincidental defense, not the
          model "seeing it coming."
        

        

          The market-neutral book is the one case with a real, mechanistic edge: it held
          LUNA short (−0.125 weight) heading into May 9, the day LUNA fell roughly
          53% in 24 hours, capturing +6.6% of portfolio return from that single day alone. But
          the same market-cap eligibility filter that protected v2/v3 then worked against the
          market-neutral book's upside: as LUNA's price (and therefore its static-supply mcap
          proxy) collapsed, it fell out of the Top-8 eligible universe entirely on May 9, zeroing
          the position for the remainder of the crash — down to fractions of a cent —
          and capping what would otherwise have been a much larger short-side gain. Our model's
          static-supply assumption only partially reflects what actually happened: LUNA's real
          circulating supply expanded roughly 16,000–18,500× in days as Terra's
          algorithm hyperinflated it defending the UST peg, while our proxy held supply constant
          and let price alone drive the (already-zeroed) ranking.
        

      

    

  


  

    

      

        
 SOL — The FTX Collapse, Re-Ranking in Real Time

        
2022-11-06 → 2022-11-15 · v3 (long-only) vs. market-neutral

      

      
CROSS-SECTIONAL RE-RANK ADAPTED WITHIN DAYS

    

    

      

        

          

        

        

          

            
SOL Price, Nov 1 → Nov 12 2022

            
$32.20 → $14.58

            
−54.7% in 12 days

          

          

            
v3 SOL Positions, Sep–Dec 2022

            
None

            
BTC bear-regime overlay kept book flat

          

          

            
MN Positioning Switch

            
Long → Short

            
flipped within the first days of the crash

          

          

            
MN SOL Leg P&L

            
+5.1%

            
contribution, Nov 1 – Dec 1 2022

          

        

      

      

        

          v3 held zero SOL exposure through the entire FTX collapse window — not
          because it identified SOL's specific counterparty risk to Alameda/FTX, but simply
          because BTC itself was below its own 200-day SMA for most of late 2022, and the BTC
          regime overlay blocks all new altcoin entries during that condition. A blunt,
          un-targeted form of protection that happened to work here.
        

        

          The market-neutral book tells a more interesting and more honest story. Its
          score actually ranked SOL among the strongest assets in the universe in the
          days just before the crash (it was briefly long SOL on Nov 1 and Nov 4–5) —
          a real miss by the momentum signal, which had no way to anticipate an
          exchange-counterparty shock. But the daily re-ranking mechanism adapted fast: by
          Nov 6, SOL's collapsing score flipped it into the short basket, where it
          stayed through the worst of the crash. The position was then closed entirely on
          Nov 11 when SOL's own price collapse pushed its market-cap proxy rank from 8 to 9,
          dropping it out of the eligible universe — the same structural interaction seen
          in the LUNA case, again capping (rather than eliminating) the gain. Net effect: a
          modest single-asset miss that the cross-sectional structure converted into a modest
          gain within days, versus a long-only book that simply never engaged.
        

      

    

  


  

    

      

        
 DOGE — The 2021 Pump-and-Dump Whipsaw

        
2021-01-01 → 2021-05-31 · v3 (long-only)

      

      
ROUND-TRIPPED A 120× RUN INTO A NET LOSS

    

    

      

        

          

        

        

          

            
DOGE Underlying Move

            
+12,045%

            
$0.0057 (Jan 1) → $0.690 (May 7 peak)

          

          

            
v3 Cumulative Round-Trip P&L

            
−18.1%

            
across 4 entry/exit episodes

          

        

        

          

            
EpisodeEntryExitReturn

            
          

        

      

      

        

          DOGE's 2021 retail-mania pump is the cleanest illustration of momentum-decay-exit
          whipsaw in the whole backtest. The breakout signal correctly identified DOGE's strength
          four separate times during a run that took it up roughly 120× — and
          lost money on net across all four episodes combined. The pattern: brief, violent
          breakout-and-reversal cycles (a single-day round trip in February, a one-day round trip
          in mid-April, a sharp reversal three days later, and a six-day hold into the May 7 peak
          that exited on the way back down) repeatedly triggered entries just ahead of local tops
          and exits just after local bottoms.
        

        

          This is not a universe-eligibility story like LUNA or SOL — DOGE was comfortably
          inside the Top-8 throughout. It is a pure signal-timing problem: a 20-day-high breakout
          with a fixed-day decay exit is well-suited to sustained, gradual trends and poorly
          suited to the violent, narrative-driven, multi-day-cycle volatility characteristic of a
          retail pump-and-dump. The strategy was directionally right about DOGE four times in a
          row and still lost money — a useful caution against equating "correct signal" with
          "profitable trade" in an asset class this volatile.
        

      

    

  





  

    09
    
Tail-Event Case Studies

    Five historical crypto crashes · exact strategy performance during each window
  


  

    For each event below, every strategy's return and max drawdown during the event window
    itself is measured directly from the backtest — not simulated or stress-tested
    after the fact. Pre-event positioning (what each strategy held the day before the window
    begins) is shown alongside, along with the average pairwise correlation across the 20-asset
    universe during the event, connecting back to Section 5's diversification-illusion finding.
  


  

  





  

    10
    
Stress-Testing the Assumptions

    Transaction costs, turnover, and parameter sensitivity
  


  

    Neither backtest models transaction costs, slippage, or funding rates. This section
    quantifies how much that matters — and how sensitive the headline results are to the
    specific parameters chosen.
  


  
10.1Turnover & Transaction Cost Sensitivity

  

    The market-neutral strategy's daily full-universe re-ranking comes at a real structural
    cost: annualized turnover of ×, versus just
    × for v3's much lower-frequency long-only book
    — an order of magnitude difference. At zero transaction costs this doesn't matter; in
    live trading, it matters enormously.
  


  

    
Cost per leg: 0 bpsdrag to recalculate live

    
    

  


  

    

      
CAGR vs. Transaction Cost (bps per leg), Market-Neutral vs. v3

      

    

    

      

        

          
Cost/legCAGRSharpeMax DDFinal Equity

          
        

      

      

        Market-neutral's edge erodes fast: a CAGR of 42.7% at zero cost falls to 11.1% at
        25bps/leg (a realistic estimate for a liquid perpetual-futures venue including funding),
        and turns negative (−13.5%) at 50bps/leg. v3, by contrast, is comparatively
        insensitive to transaction costs — its much lower turnover means even 100bps/leg
        only modestly compresses its returns. This is a first-order consideration for live
        deployment, not a footnote: at realistic cost assumptions, market-neutral's edge could
        be eliminated by execution costs alone.
      

    

  


  

    NOT MODELED Perpetual futures funding-rate carry on the
    market-neutral strategy's short leg (which can run persistently negative during strong bull
    regimes, eroding returns further), bid-ask spread on smaller-cap names during stress windows
    (likely worse than the flat bps estimates above precisely when correlation spikes, per
    Section 5), and any market-impact cost from the strategy's own trading.
  


  
10.2Parameter Sensitivity — v3 (LOOKBACK × TRAIL_STOP)

  

    Holding all other v3 parameters fixed, varying the breakout lookback window and trailing
    stop percentage produces a meaningfully different Sharpe ratio across the grid (1.03 to
    1.51) — and the actually-deployed configuration (LOOKBACK=20, TRAIL_STOP=15%, Sharpe
    1.29) is not the best Sharpe in the grid. LOOKBACK=25 with a wider 20–25%
    trailing stop would have scored better in-sample. This is disclosed deliberately: the
    chosen parameters were not the result of a grid search for the best Sharpe, and a strategy
    whose parameters were chosen this way would carry meaningful overfitting risk that
    this report does not attempt to correct for (see Section 12).
  

  

    
v3 Sharpe Ratio Heatmap — Breakout Lookback × Trailing Stop %

    

  


  
10.3Parameter Sensitivity — Market-Neutral (TOP_N × M)

  

    The market-neutral grid is far more volatile: Sharpe ranges from 0.33 to 1.65
    depending on universe breadth (TOP_N) and leg size (M). The deployed configuration
    (TOP_N=8, M=4) happens to be the single best Sharpe in the tested grid — reassuring,
    but also a reason for caution: TOP_N=16, M=2 (a thin 2-name-per-leg book drawn from a wide
    16-asset eligible pool) produced a catastrophic −99.2% max drawdown, an order of
    magnitude worse than the deployed configuration's −24.6%. Small leg counts concentrate
    idiosyncratic risk in exactly the way the long-only architecture's inverse-vol sizing was
    built to avoid (Section 4.4) — the market-neutral architecture has no equivalent
    safeguard.
  

  

    
Market-Neutral Sharpe Ratio Heatmap — TOP_N × M

    

  





  

    11
    
Diagnosis

    What each architecture got right and wrong — applied to both strategies
  


  
11.1Strategy 1 (Long-Only Breakout) — Final Assessment

  

    

      
THE SIGNAL WAS NEVER THE PROBLEM

      

        Across all three iterations, the 20-day-high breakout signal correctly identified strong
        momentum repeatedly (Sections 4, 8). v1's raw CAGR of 46.6% shows the signal carries real
        edge. Every change across v1→v2→v3 was a change to risk architecture
        (universe size, exits, sizing) — the alpha source itself was untouched and never
        the cause of underperformance.
      

    

    

      
RISK CONTROLS WORKED EXACTLY AS DESIGNED

      

        The progression from v1 to v3 cut max drawdown by 80% (−70.4% →
        −14.4%) through legitimate, well-understood mechanisms: a trailing stop that
        reacts faster than a 200-day SMA, a BTC regime overlay that recognizes systemic
        risk-off conditions, and inverse-vol sizing that fixed the sum-to-1 normalization flaw
        directly. Each addition is independently defensible and the LUNA/SOL case studies
        (Section 8) show the eligibility filter doing real, structural protective work.
      

    

    

      
BUT THE ARCHITECTURE HAS A HARD CEILING

      

        Long-only exposure on a universe with ~0.64 baseline pairwise correlation (Section 5)
        means every risk reduction mechanically caps upside, with no offsetting mechanism to
        monetize the other side of any trade. v3's final CAGR of 15.0% trails simple BTC
        buy-and-hold's 31.2% — the strategy successfully protected capital, but at the
        cost of most of its edge over doing nothing at all.
      

    

    

      
SIGNAL-TIMING MISMATCH IN VOLATILE REGIMES

      

        The DOGE case study (Section 8) shows a fixed-day momentum-decay exit is poorly matched
        to violent, narrative-driven, multi-day-cycle pump-and-dump behavior — the
        strategy round-tripped a 120× underlying move into a net −18.1% loss across
        four separate (individually correct) entries. The signal worked; the exit timing did not
        match this asset's volatility character.
      

    

  


  
11.2Strategy 2 (Market-Neutral) — Final Assessment

  

    

      
CROSS-SECTIONAL REFRAME SOLVED THE CEILING

      

        Re-applying the same momentum score relatively instead of absolutely let the strategy
        monetize both strong and weak assets simultaneously, breaking the long-only
        return ceiling: CAGR 42.7% vs. v3's 15.0%, Sharpe 1.65 vs. 1.29, and near-zero realized
        correlation to BTC (−0.12 vs. v3's 0.39). The SOL/FTX case study (Section 8) shows
        the daily re-ranking mechanism adapting to a fast-moving shock within days.
      

    

    

      
BUT IT IS NOT A FREE LUNCH

      

        Max drawdown (−24.6%) is deeper than v3's (−14.4%), and the strategy carries
        zero position-level circuit breakers — no trailing stop, no SMA filter, no
        regime overlay of any kind (Section 6.1). It relies entirely on the long-short
        construction and daily re-ranking to manage risk, an architecture choice that has not
        been stress-tested against a scenario where the cross-sectional ranking itself breaks
        down (e.g., a market-wide flight to a single "safe" asset).
      

    

    

      
TRANSACTION-COST FRAGILITY IS A REAL, FIRST-ORDER RISK

      

        At ~99.7× annualized turnover, the market-neutral strategy's edge is highly
        sensitive to execution costs (Section 10.1): CAGR falls from 42.7% to 11.1% at a
        realistic 25bps/leg, and turns negative above 50bps/leg. None of the headline numbers in
        this report should be read as achievable returns without a careful, realistic
        transaction-cost model layered on top.
      

    

    

      
SAME STATIC-SUPPLY MARKET-CAP FLAW, NEW FAILURE MODE

      

        Both the LUNA and SOL case studies (Section 8) show the same market-cap eligibility
        filter interacting perversely with the strategy's own short positions: as a shorted
        asset's price (and therefore its static-supply mcap proxy) collapses, it falls out of
        the eligible universe and the position is forcibly closed — capping the very
        downside-capture trade the short leg exists to make.
      

    

  


  
11.3Net Assessment

  

    Both architectures share the same alpha source and the same data limitations (static-supply
    market-cap proxy, no transaction costs modeled, no funding-rate carry). The decisive
    difference is exposure construction: long-only momentum, however carefully risk-managed,
    cannot escape a structural ceiling on returns in a market this internally correlated; dollar-
    neutral cross-sectional construction breaks through that ceiling but trades it for a
    different, transaction-cost-sensitive risk profile with materially fewer built-in safeguards.
    The pivot from Strategy 1 to Strategy 2 is a genuine improvement in risk-adjusted return and
    diversification — not a strictly safer or more robust system in every dimension.
  





  

    12
    
Proposed Improvements, Ranked by Impact

    8 items · derived from Sections 5, 8, 10 & 11
  


  

    

      
1

      

        
Replace the static-supply market-cap proxy with real historical market-cap data

        

          The single most concrete, recurring flaw in this report: the static-supply assumption
          let LUNA's eligibility collapse correctly track its price decline but completely missed
          the ~16,000–18,500× real supply expansion (Section 8), and caused both the
          LUNA short and SOL short legs in the market-neutral strategy to be forcibly closed by
          falling out of the Top-8 universe right as they became most informative (Section 8,
          10). A paid data vendor's true historical circulating-supply series would fix this at
          its root for both strategies simultaneously.
        

      

      
HIGH IMPACT
data quality

    


    

      
2

      

        
Model transaction costs, slippage, and perpetual funding-rate carry before any live deployment

        

          Section 10.1 shows the market-neutral strategy's CAGR falls from 42.7% to 11.1% at a
          realistic 25bps/leg and turns negative above 50bps/leg, given ~99.7× annualized
          turnover. None of this report's headline numbers should be read as achievable returns
          without this layered on top, and funding-rate carry on the short leg (which can run
          persistently negative in strong bull regimes) is entirely unmodeled.
        

      

      
HIGH IMPACT
execution realism

    


    

      
3

      

        
Add portfolio-level risk budgeting on top of per-position sizing

        

          v3's inverse-vol sizing manages each position's idiosyncratic vol but has no
          mechanism to recognize that the whole 8-asset book can still move together (Section 5's
          ~0.64 baseline correlation, spiking to 0.80–0.96 in tail events). A portfolio-
          level vol target — scaling gross exposure down when realized cross-asset
          correlation spikes, for both strategies — would directly address the
          diversification-illusion finding rather than only managing each leg in isolation.
        

      

      
HIGH IMPACT
risk architecture

    


    

      
4

      

        
Move from dollar-neutral to beta-neutral leg construction

        

          The market-neutral book is dollar-neutral (50% long / 50% short) by construction but
          only approximately beta-neutral in practice (realized BTC correlation of
          −0.12, not exactly zero). Explicitly weighting each leg by inverse beta-to-BTC,
          rather than equal-weighting within each leg, would tighten this further and make the
          "market-neutral" label more literally true.
        

      

      
MEDIUM IMPACT
model design

    


    

      
5

      

        
Add a position-level circuit breaker to the market-neutral book

        

          Section 6.1 and Section 10.3's parameter grid both flag the same gap: the
          market-neutral architecture has zero per-position stop-loss or trailing-stop logic,
          relying entirely on next-day re-ranking. The TOP_N=16/M=2 sensitivity result
          (−99.2% max drawdown) shows how badly this can fail when leg concentration is
          high; a simple per-leg stop would add a safeguard the long-only architecture already
          has and the market-neutral one does not.
        

      

      
MEDIUM IMPACT
risk architecture

    


    

      
6

      

        
Calibrate the exit window to realized volatility regime, not a fixed day-count

        

          The DOGE case study (Section 8) shows a fixed 5-day momentum-decay exit whipsawing
          through a violent, narrative-driven pump-and-dump — round-tripping a 120×
          underlying move into a net loss. A volatility-adaptive exit window (shorter holds when
          realized vol is elevated) would better match exit timing to each asset's current
          behavior rather than using one fixed rule for both calm trends and violent mania cycles.
        

      

      
MEDIUM IMPACT
signal design

    


    

      
7

      

        
Run proper walk-forward / out-of-sample validation given the v1→v2→v3 search process

        

          Section 10.2–10.3 show in-sample parameter sensitivity grids where the deployed
          configuration is sometimes (v3) and sometimes not (market-neutral) the best-performing
          cell — and the project's own three-iteration search process (v1→v2→v3)
          is itself a form of parameter search conducted with full knowledge of the backtest
          outcome. An honest out-of-sample or walk-forward re-validation, ideally on data the
          model has never seen, is the appropriate next step before treating any of these Sharpe
          ratios as forward-looking estimates.
        

      

      
LOW IMPACT
methodology rigor

    


    

      
8

      

        
Add on-chain and funding-rate signals as a second factor alongside price momentum

        

          Both strategies rely exclusively on price-derived momentum. On-chain metrics (exchange
          netflows, active addresses, stablecoin supply growth) and perpetual funding-rate
          extremes (a direct measure of leveraged positioning, relevant to three of the five
          tail events in Section 9) could provide an independent confirming or contradicting
          signal, particularly around leverage-driven liquidation cascades that price momentum
          alone reacts to only after the fact.
        

      

      
LOW IMPACT
new data source

    

  





      

        
RISK-PARITY BREAKOUT RESEARCH LOG · From 20-Day Breakout to Cross-Sectional Market-Neutral Momentum

        
Backtest only · not investment advice · transaction costs and funding rates not included in headline figures
